title: TOCTOU race condition in upsert_property()
description: The upsert_property() method performs a SELECT to check for existing records, then either UPDATEs or INSERTs. Under concurrent access, two callers can both see no existing record and both attempt INSERT, causing a unique constraint violation on (portal_source, source_id). While the DB constraint prevents duplicate rows, the is_new return value becomes unreliable and one caller will get an exception instead of a clean update.
suggestedFix: Use PostgreSQL's ON CONFLICT (portal_source, source_id) DO UPDATE SET ... RETURNING to perform atomic upsert. For SQLite compatibility, keep the current pattern but document the limitation. Alternatively, catch the unique constraint violation and retry as an update.

title: TOCTOU race in ID generation for SQLite fallback
description: Lines 209-214 use max(id) + 1 to generate IDs for SQLite. This is a classic race condition: two concurrent transactions can read the same max ID and both attempt to insert with the same ID, causing a primary key violation.
suggestedFix: Remove the manual ID generation. Use GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY on PostgreSQL (already in migration) and let SQLite use AUTOINCREMENT via Integer primary_key=True with autoincrement=True. The model already has autoincrement=False which prevents SQLite auto-assignment; change to autoincrement=True for SQLite compatibility.

title: PostGIS type mismatch: migration uses GEOMETRY, model uses Geometry
description: The migration creates the location column as GEOMETRY(Point, 4326) (line 71) but the SQLAlchemy model uses geoalchemy2.Geometry('POINT', srid=4326). While these are compatible in PostGIS, the case difference (Point vs POINT) and the raw SQL vs ORM type declaration create maintenance risk and potential drift.
suggestedFix: Align both to use GEOMETRY(POINT, 4326) consistently. In the migration, use geoalchemy2.Geometry('POINT', srid=4326) via the ORM or ensure the raw SQL matches the model's type exactly. The model's geoalchemy2.Geometry('POINT', srid=4326) is the canonical definition.

title: Pipeline does not wait for database availability
description: The open_spider() method creates the async engine and session factory but does not call wait_for_db() to verify connectivity before proceeding. If PostgreSQL is not ready, the first process_item() will fail with a connection error instead of retrying.
suggestedFix: Call await wait_for_db(self._db_engine) after creating the engine in open_spider(), before creating the session factory. This uses the existing retry logic with exponential backoff.

title: Unused get_db_session() function
description: The get_db_session() async context manager is defined but never used anywhere in the codebase. The pipeline creates sessions directly via self._session_factory(). Dead code increases maintenance burden and confusion.
suggestedFix: Either remove get_db_session() if not needed, or refactor pipeline.py to use it for consistent session management. Given the pipeline's lifecycle (open_spider/close_spider), the current direct session factory usage is appropriate; remove the unused function.

title: Logging tests do not validate actual log output
description: Tests in test_logging.py only verify that logging calls don't crash (assert True). They don't capture or validate the JSON format, field presence (timestamp, level, message, portal, scraper_id, run_id), or bound logger behavior. This provides false confidence.
suggestedFix: Use pytest's capsys fixture or io.StringIO to capture stdout/stderr, then parse and validate the JSON log output. Verify all required fields are present and correctly formatted.

title: Weak timestamp assertion in test_upsert_existing
description: Line 41 only asserts prop2.last_seen_at is not None. It doesn't verify that last_seen_at was actually refreshed (updated to a newer timestamp) on the upsert. The test would pass even if the timestamp wasn't updated.
suggestedFix: Capture the original last_seen_at before the upsert, then assert the new last_seen_at is greater than the original (allowing for clock resolution). Use a small sleep or compare with datetime.now(UTC) before/after.

title: Mypy error: Returning Any from function declared to return BoundLogger
description: The get_logger() function returns structlog.get_logger().bind(...) which mypy infers as Any, but the return type annotation is structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger.
suggestedFix: Add explicit type annotation to the bind() call or cast the return value: return cast(structlog.stdlib.BoundLogger, structlog.get_logger().bind(...))

title: Mypy error: Missing type arguments for generic type dict
description: Fields extras, photos, localization, building use dict without type arguments (e.g., dict[str, Any]). This violates strict mypy configuration.
suggestedFix: Add type arguments: Mapped[dict[str, Any] Any] Any] | None = mapped_column(JSONB, nullable=True) for each JSONB field.

title: Mypy error: Unused type: ignore comment
description: Line 107 has # type: ignore[arg-type] but the error it was suppressing has been resolved (likely by the geoalchemy2 patch in conftest.py). The ignore comment is now unnecessary.
suggestedFix: Remove the # type: ignore[arg-type] comment on line 107.

title: Plan task checkboxes not updated for completed phases
description: Phase 5 tasks (5.1-5.10) are shown as unchecked [ ] but the execution log says Phase 5 is 'completed'. Phase 7 tasks are also unchecked but the phase is 'pending'. This creates confusion about actual progress.
suggestedFix: Update all Phase 5 task checkboxes to [x]. Update Phase 7 tasks to [ ] (pending) or [x] as appropriate. Update ruff line-length in Phase 5 task 5.9 from 100 to 120 to match pyproject.toml.

title: Pipeline uses Any type for spider parameter
description: The open_spider, close_spider, and process_item methods use Any for the spider parameter instead of the proper Scrapy Spider type. This reduces type safety and IDE support.
suggestedFix: Import Spider from scrapy and use it as the type annotation: from scrapy import Spider; then def open_spider(self, spider: Spider) -> None:

title: Property.id type differs from spec (Integer vs UUID)
description: The spec (F-2) states Property.id should be UUID PK, but the implementation uses Integer with GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY. The migration also uses INTEGER with IDENTITY. This is a deliberate design choice (performance) but diverges from the spec.
suggestedFix: Either update the spec to reflect Integer PK (with rationale: performance, partitioning compatibility) or change the implementation to UUID. Given LIST partitioning requires the partition key in the PK, Integer is a reasonable choice. Document the decision.

title: db_utils.py has only two functions, could be merged
description: The db_utils.py module contains only check_connection() and property_count(). These could be merged into database.py to reduce module count.
suggestedFix: Move check_connection() to database.py (it's already imported there) and property_count() to services.py or remove if unused. Then delete db_utils.py.

title: Partition documentation could be more explicit
description: The migration has a comment about adding partitions for new portal sources (lines 97-99) but it's brief. Could include a concrete example and reference to the DEFAULT partition behavior.
suggestedFix: Expand the comment with a step-by-step example: 'To add a new portal source: 1) ALTER TABLE properties ATTACH PARTITION properties_<new> FOR VALUES IN ('<new>'); 2) The DEFAULT partition (properties_other) catches unknown sources.'
